_comment: Web-verified attribution-signal ruleset (June 2026). Generated from the catalog sweep. Temporal/signature/identity-field detection is handled by builtin capability checks (see engine/builtins.ts), not by these data-driven rules.

rules.title: Claude Code Co-Authored-By trailer
rules.fix: Strip the trailing Co-Authored-By: Claude trailer line from the commit message before the push reaches the remote. Optionally tell the dev to set includeCoAuthoredBy=false in ~/.claude/settings.json.

rules.title: Claude Code "Generated with Claude Code" robot footer
rules.fix: Remove the robot-emoji 'Generated with [Claude Code]' line from the commit body and from any PR description body created via gh pr create.

rules.title: Claude Code second Co-authored-by leaking human Anthropic account email
rules.fix: Since Claude Code v2.1.165 (2026-06-05) a second Co-authored-by trailer carrying the human's Anthropic account email is injected even when git author.email is a noreply. Strip any extra Co-authored-by trailer that appears alongside the Claude trailer; verify against the intended author identity to avoid removing a legitimate human co-author.

rules.title: CLAUDE.md / CLAUDE.local.md memory file
rules.fix: CLAUDE.md is Claude Code's project memory/instruction file and reveals the AI workflow. Either add to .gitignore or strip from the push if attribution scrubbing is required.

rules.title: .claude/ config directory (settings, commands, agents)
rules.fix: The .claude/ directory (settings.json, settings.local.json, commands/, agents/) marks a Claude Code workflow. Exclude it from pushes or .gitignore it. settings.local.json should never be committed.

rules.title: Claude Code session transcripts / history (~/.claude)
rules.fix: Claude Code stores per-session JSONL transcripts at ~/.claude/projects/<slug>/<uuid>.jsonl and a global ~/.claude/history.jsonl index. These normally live outside the repo; if they appear in a commit, drop them — they leak full prompt logs.

rules.title: GitHub Copilot Co-authored-by trailer (IDE git.addAICoAuthor)
rules.fix: VS Code's git.addAICoAuthor setting appends this trailer. Strip the 'Co-authored-by: Copilot <copilot@github.com>' line; advise setting git.addAICoAuthor to 'off'.

rules.title: Copilot coding-agent bot Co-authored-by / author email
rules.fix: The Copilot coding agent commits/PRs under copilot-swe-agent[bot] with a <id>+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com email. Rewrite author/committer identity to the human and strip the bot Co-authored-by trailer.

rules.title: .github/copilot-instructions.md custom instructions
rules.fix: Repository-wide Copilot instruction file that signals a Copilot workflow. Exclude or .gitignore if scrubbing tool attribution.

rules.title: Copilot path-specific .instructions.md files
rules.fix: Path-scoped Copilot instruction files (with applyTo frontmatter) under .github/instructions/. Strip or ignore to remove the Copilot workflow signal.

rules.title: Cursor Agent Co-authored-by trailer
rules.fix: Cursor's agent appends this co-author trailer by default. Strip it; advise disabling in Cursor Settings > Agent > Attribution.

rules.title: Cursor "Made with Cursor" commit/PR trailer
rules.fix: Cursor can add a 'Made with Cursor' trailer to commits and gh-created PRs. Remove the line from commit bodies and PR descriptions.

rules.title: Cursor project rules (.cursor/rules/*.mdc) and legacy .cursorrules
rules.fix: Cursor rule files (.cursor/rules/*.mdc and legacy .cursorrules) plus .cursorignore reveal the Cursor workflow. Exclude or .gitignore them.

rules.title: Aider "(aider)" author/committer name suffix
rules.fix: Aider appends '(aider)' to the git author and/or committer name. Rewrite the name to drop the '(aider)' suffix; advise --attribute-author=false / --attribute-committer=false.

rules.title: Aider Co-authored-by trailer with model name
rules.fix: Aider appends a Co-authored-by trailer naming the model and noreply@aider.chat (default on). Strip the trailer; advise --attribute-co-authored-by=false.

rules.title: Aider config / chat-history / input-history files
rules.fix: Aider drops .aider.conf.yml plus prompt/session artifacts (.aider.chat.history.md, .aider.input.history, .aider.tags.cache.v*) into the repo. Add the .aider* glob to .gitignore and strip from pushes — the history files contain full prompt logs.

rules.title: OpenAI Codex Co-authored-by trailer
rules.fix: Codex's commit_attribution feature appends 'Co-authored-by: Codex <noreply@openai.com>'. Strip the trailer; advise setting commit_attribution to empty in ~/.codex/config.toml.

rules.title: Codex default placeholder git identity (codex@example.com)
rules.fix: Codex ships a default identity user.name='Codex Test' / user.email='codex@example.com' (RFC2606 doc domain, now squatted by a real GitHub user). Rewrite author/committer to the intended human identity before pushing.

rules.title: Codex home/config artifacts (~/.codex, AGENTS.override.md)
rules.fix: Codex reads from ~/.codex (config.toml, AGENTS.md/AGENTS.override.md). If these appear inside the repo, exclude them — they reveal the Codex workflow.

rules.title: AGENTS.md agent-instruction file (Codex/Cursor/Copilot/etc.)
rules.fix: AGENTS.md is the cross-vendor agent-instruction convention read by Codex, Cursor, Copilot coding agent and others. It signals an AI-agent workflow but is not tied to one tool — flag as a generic workflow signal; exclude/.gitignore if scrubbing.

rules.title: Windsurf .windsurfrules project rules file
rules.fix: Strip .windsurfrules from the push; it reveals the repo was driven by Windsurf/Cascade and may encode workflow preferences. Move guidance into a tool-neutral doc if needed.

rules.title: Windsurf .windsurf/rules and global_rules.md
rules.fix: Remove the .windsurf/rules/ directory and global_rules.md from the tree before push; global_rules.md is a generic name so confirm context. These are Windsurf-specific rule artifacts.

rules.title: Codeium/Windsurf Cascade memories dir
rules.fix: Block: ~/.codeium/windsurf/memories/ is machine-local Cascade state and should never be committed. Its presence is strong evidence of attribution leakage; remove entirely.

rules.title: Continue.dev .continue/ config directory
rules.fix: Strip the .continue/ directory (config.yaml, rules/, prompts/, assistants/, mcpServers/) before push; it ties the repo to Continue.dev and may carry model/workflow config.

rules.title: Continue.dev legacy .continuerules file
rules.fix: Remove the legacy .continuerules raw-text rules file; it identifies Continue.dev usage.

rules.title: Gemini CLI GEMINI.md context file
rules.fix: GEMINI.md is the Gemini CLI hierarchical context file. Often intentionally committed, but it discloses Gemini CLI usage; scrub or rename to a neutral doc if attribution must be hidden.

rules.title: Gemini CLI / Code Assist .gemini directory
rules.fix: Strip .gemini/ (settings.json for the CLI; config.yaml + styleguide.md for the Code Assist GitHub app). Reveals Gemini tooling and review automation.

rules.title: Gemini Code Assist bot co-author/identity
rules.fix: Rewrite/strip any commit author, committer, or Co-authored-by trailer referencing gemini-code-assist[bot]. Note it commonly originates from the GitHub review app rather than authored code.

rules.title: Google Jules bot author/co-author
rules.fix: Rewrite author/committer/Co-authored-by trailers that reference google-labs-jules[bot]; Jules can be configured (since Feb 2026) to commit as the user, co-author, or itself.

rules.title: Devin AI Co-authored-by trailer
rules.fix: Strip the Devin AI Co-authored-by trailer from commit messages before push.

rules.title: devin-ai-integration[bot] author/committer identity
rules.fix: Rewrite commit author/committer identity off devin-ai-integration[bot] (the Devin GitHub App actor) to the human owner.

rules.title: Devin/Cognition .devin/rules directory
rules.fix: Remove .devin/rules/ (used by Devin and newer Cognition/Windsurf builds) before push; it discloses Cognition tooling.

rules.title: Sourcegraph Cody .sourcegraph/*.rule.md
rules.fix: Strip .sourcegraph/*.rule.md rule files; they identify Sourcegraph Cody / code-review-agent usage and encode workflow standards.

rules.title: Sourcegraph Cody .cody/ignore file
rules.fix: Optionally remove .cody/ignore (Cody context-exclusion file); low-sensitivity but reveals Cody usage.

rules.title: Amazon Q Developer .amazonq/rules
rules.fix: Remove .amazonq/rules/*.md project rule files (Amazon Q Developer, formerly CodeWhisperer) before push.

rules.title: Tabnine .tabnine agent config
rules.fix: Strip .tabnine/agent/settings.json and .tabnine/mcp_servers.json; these are project-scoped Tabnine agent config files that disclose tool usage.

rules.title: JetBrains Junie .junie/guidelines.md
rules.fix: Remove the .junie/ directory (guidelines.md / guidelines/ folder, mcp.json) before push; it identifies JetBrains Junie usage.

rules.title: JetBrains AI Assistant .aiassistant/rules + .aiignore
rules.fix: Strip .aiassistant/rules/*.md, .aiignore, and .noai (JetBrains AI Assistant artifacts); they disclose JetBrains AI usage and AI-exclusion intent.

rules.title: Replit Agent replit.md context file
rules.fix: replit.md is auto-generated by Replit Agent to capture project context/preferences. Often committed intentionally, but it reveals Replit Agent authorship; scrub if hiding attribution.

rules.title: Replit .replit config file
rules.fix: The .replit file configures the Replit environment/run commands; it signals the project was built on Replit. Low sensitivity but a workflow tell.

rules.title: Cline .clinerules file or directory
rules.fix: Strip .clinerules (single file or directory of .md files) before push; it identifies Cline agent usage and encodes workflow rules.

rules.title: Roo Code .roo/rules and .roorules
rules.fix: Remove .roo/rules/, .roo/rules-{mode}/, .roorules, .roorules-{mode}, and .roomodes (Roo Code; shut down May 2026 but legacy files persist in repos).

rules.title: bolt.new .bolt/ project artifacts
rules.fix: Strip the .bolt/ directory (prompt = custom project instructions injected every chat; ignore = context exclusions; config.json); it discloses bolt.new (StackBlitz) usage.

rules.title: Augment .augment-guidelines and .augment/rules
rules.fix: Remove .augment-guidelines and .augment/rules/ files (Augment Code workspace guidelines) before push.

rules.title: Zed AI .rules worktree instructions file
rules.fix: The .rules file at a worktree root is Zed's project-level agent instruction file. Generic name (also read by other tools) so confirm context; scrub if hiding agent attribution.

rules.title: AGENTS.md shared agent instruction standard
rules.fix: AGENTS.md / AGENT.md is the cross-tool agent-instruction standard adopted by v0/Vercel, Zed, Junie, Gemini CLI, Amp, Codex and others. Very often committed deliberately; flag as informational only. Removing it hides that an agent workflow is in use but breaks legitimate setups.

rules.title: Co-authored-by trailer (person attribution)
rules.fix: Strip the entire Co-authored-by trailer line(s) from the commit message before push. These directly bind a named/emailed person to the commit and are surfaced on GitHub/GitLab contribution graphs.

rules.title: Co-authored-by with GitHub noreply email (account-linkable)
rules.fix: Remove the trailer. Both the modern ID+username@users.noreply.github.com and legacy username@users.noreply.github.com forms resolve to a specific GitHub account and link the commit to that profile.

rules.title: Signed-off-by (DCO) trailer
rules.fix: Strip Signed-off-by lines (added by `git commit -s`). They carry a real name + email asserting Developer Certificate of Origin and are pure person attribution. Note: removing may break DCO-required CI checks on the remote.

rules.title: Reviewed-by / Acked-by / Tested-by trailers
rules.fix: Remove these reviewer/approver trailers. They name additional people involved in the workflow (reviewer, acker, tester) with name+email.

rules.title: Reported-by / Suggested-by / Requested-by / Helped-by / Thanks-to credit trailers
rules.fix: Strip credit-style trailers naming a person. Same person-attribution risk as Co-authored-by; commonly used in kernel/large OSS workflows.

rules.title: Generic '*-by:' trailer carrying an email address
rules.fix: Catch-all for any 'Xxx-by:' attribution trailer containing an <email>. Strip the line. Broader than the named-trailer rules to catch project-specific variants (Created-by, Validated-by, Approved-by, etc.).

rules.title: Bare email address in commit message body
rules.example: asked maintainer alice.smith@bigcorp.com to double-check the migration
rules.fix: Flag/redact email addresses appearing in the free-text body (not just trailers). High false-positive risk: code snippets, config samples, and placeholder emails (example.com) match. Exclude common placeholder domains and review before auto-redacting.

rules.title: @username mention (GitHub/GitLab handle)
rules.example: thanks @torvalds for the pointer on the locking fix
rules.fix: Flag @handle mentions, which notify and link a specific account on the forge. High false-positive risk: email local parts, npm scopes (@scope/pkg), Twitter-style handles, and decorators (@Override). Negative lookbehind for word/@/slash reduces noise; review before stripping.

rules.title: 'On <date>, <name> wrote:' email-reply attribution line
rules.example: On Tue, 17 Jun 2026 at 14:02, Jane Developer <jane@example.com> wrote:
rules.fix: Strip pasted email-quote attribution lines (and the quoted block beneath, often '>'-prefixed). These leak a name/date/email from mailing-list or email-patch workflows pasted into the message.

rules.title: Debian changelog maintainer trailer ('-- name <email>  date')
rules.fix: Strip/anonymize Debian-style changelog signature lines if they appear in commit text or staged debian/changelog diffs. Format is '-- maintainer <email>  RFC2822-date' with exactly two spaces before the date.

rules.title: Changelog/release-notes author attribution (by <name> / (@handle))
rules.example: Fixed the off-by-one in the parser (by Jane Developer)
rules.fix: Flag changelog-style author credits ('by Jane Developer', 'Thanks to @handle', 'patch by ...'). High false-positive risk on ordinary prose ('handled by the scheduler'); treat as review-then-redact, not auto-block.

rules.title: Issue/PR/Jira ticket reference (traceable to a person/workflow)
rules.fix: Flag issue/PR (#123, GH-123) and Jira-style (ABC-123) references. They map back to an internal tracker, reporter, and assignee, deanonymizing the workflow. High FP risk: ABC-123 matches version strings, model numbers, and SI units (e.g., UTF-8, RFC-822); scope by issue keywords or known project prefixes to reduce noise.

rules.title: Automated-generation marker / 'Generated with' attribution line
rules.example: 🤖 Generated with an AI coding assistant
rules.fix: Strip 'Generated with/by ...' attribution lines (often robot-emoji-prefixed) that flag automated authorship and tie the commit to a generation workflow. Tool-agnostic; pair with the tool-specific catalog for named-product trailers.

rules.title: Robot/AI emoji marker in commit message
rules.fix: Flag the robot emoji (U+1F916) and AI-adjacent markers (brain U+1F9E0; sparkles U+2728 when near generation words). Presence is a recognized proxy for AI/automation-generated commits. Review before stripping to avoid clobbering intentional Gitmoji usage.

rules.title: 'on behalf of' / 'committed by' workflow attribution
rules.detect: \b(?:on behalf of|committed by|pushed by|cherry[-\s]?picked from commit|backported by|merged by)\b[\s:]*[^\n]*
rules.example: cherry picked from commit a1b2c3d (committed by Jane Developer)
rules.fix: Flag phrases that attribute the commit/action to a named person or upstream commit (on behalf of, committed by, cherry-picked from commit <sha>). These leak the actor and the source-branch workflow.

rules.title: @author / @authors doc-comment tag naming a person
rules.fix: Strip the @author/@authors line from the doc comment, or replace the value with a neutral placeholder (e.g. the project/org name). JSDoc, Javadoc and TSDoc all use this exact tag and render the trailing name (and any <email>) into generated docs.

rules.title: Doxygen \author / \authors comment tag
rules.fix: Doxygen accepts both backslash (\author) and at-sign (@author) tag forms. Remove or neutralize the name following the tag before push.

rules.title: "Author:" / "Created by:" labeled comment line
rules.fix: Free-text 'Author:'/'Created by:'/'Maintainer:' header lines in source comments leak a real identity. Remove the line or replace the value with an org/team name. Medium FP risk: 'Author:' can appear in quoted prose, changelog templates, or bibliography strings; scope to comment lines and file headers to reduce noise.

rules.title: "// by <name>" inline attribution
rules.fix: Inline 'by <Name>' attributions in comments name a person. High FP risk: 'by' is a common English word ('sort by Name', 'filter by Region', 'split by line'), so require a comment lead-in plus a capitalized proper-noun pattern and prefer manual review before stripping.

rules.title: TODO(name) / FIXME(name) / HACK(name) attributed marker
rules.example: // TODO(jlee): handle the null case before release
rules.fix: The parenthesized handle/username in TODO(...)/FIXME(...) markers is a Google-style code-owner attribution that ties the line to a person. Strip the (name) so it becomes a bare 'TODO:'. Medium FP risk: the parens can hold a non-identity tag like TODO(perf) or FIXME(#123); cross-check the captured token against a username/handle shape.

rules.title: Email address embedded in file content or comment
rules.fix: Personal email addresses in code/comments directly identify an individual. Replace with a role alias (e.g. team@example.com) or remove. High FP risk: matches legitimate non-identity uses (example.com placeholders, test fixtures, RFC-822 docs, schema sample data, '@' in code that is not really email); allowlist example/test/noreply domains and known placeholder strings.

rules.title: Phone number in code or comment
rules.fix: Phone numbers in source can identify an individual. Remove or replace with a non-personal contact. High FP risk: collides with version strings, timestamps, IDs, dimensions, and other numeric data; require phone-like separators and word boundaries, and prefer review over auto-strip.

rules.title: @handle social/VCS mention in content
rules.fix: An @handle (GitHub/Twitter/X/Slack username) maps to a real account. Strip or genericize attribution mentions in comments and docs. High FP risk: '@' is used for decorators (@property), npm scopes (@org/pkg), email local parts, JSDoc tags (@param), and Java/TS annotations; only flag standalone @handle tokens in prose/comments, not code constructs.

rules.title: AUTHORS / CONTRIBUTORS roster file
rules.fix: These conventional roster files exist specifically to list real names and emails of contributors. Drop the file from the push, or rewrite entries to org/team identities. The proxy should also scan their contents for name <email> lines.

rules.title: .mailmap canonical name/email mapping
rules.fix: A .mailmap at the repo toplevel maps commit names/emails to canonical real names and addresses (form: 'Proper Name <proper@email> Commit Name <commit@email>'). It is a dense identity ledger. Remove it from the push or it directly re-attributes scrubbed commits.

rules.title: .mailmap name/email mapping line
rules.fix: Each non-comment .mailmap line carries one or more 'Name <email>' identities. When scanning a .mailmap, strip or rewrite every such line. Medium FP risk: this generic 'text <email>' shape also matches AUTHORS files, package manifests, and comments, so scope it to .mailmap/AUTHORS context.

rules.title: package.json author/contributors with name<email>
rules.fix: npm 'author'/'contributors'/'maintainers' fields accept either a 'Name <email> (url)' string or a {name,email,url} object/array. Replace with an org identity or remove. Published to the registry verbatim, so this leaks on every npm publish too.

rules.title: pyproject.toml authors/maintainers (PEP 621) tables
rules.fix: PEP 621 [project] authors/maintainers are arrays of inline tables with name and email keys (Poetry's [tool.poetry] uses a 'Name <email>' string array instead). Both surface real identities into PyPI metadata. Genericize or remove.

rules.title: Poetry [tool.poetry] authors/maintainers string array
rules.fix: Legacy Poetry uses a 'Name <email>' string-array form (distinct from PEP 621 tables). Strip the email/name or replace with an org entry before push.

rules.title: Cargo.toml authors field with name<email>
rules.fix: Cargo's package.authors is an array of 'Name <email>' strings (now optional; cargo init omits it). Remove or genericize. Note it is also baked into the CARGO_PKG_AUTHORS env var at build time.

rules.title: Generic author/maintainer key in TOML/YAML/manifest metadata
rules.fix: Catches author/maintainer keys in other ecosystems' manifests (gemspec, composer.json, pubspec.yaml, setup.cfg, gradle, podspec, GitHub Action action.yml 'author'). Genericize the value. Medium FP risk: 'author' keys also legitimately appear in data/config that is not the committer's identity.

rules.title: Committed .gitconfig with user.name / user.email
rules.fix: A committed .gitconfig / .git/config [user] block sets the exact name/email used to author commits. Remove the file or strip the [user] section. Medium FP risk: 'name =' / 'email =' keys appear in unrelated INI/TOML; require the [user] section context for high confidence.

rules.title: Committed git config file
rules.fix: A .gitconfig should never be committed; it commonly contains [user] name/email plus signing keys and aliases tying the repo to a person. Remove from the push.

rules.title: .npmrc with init-author-name / init-author-email
rules.fix: npm config keys init-author-name / init-author-email / init-author-url (also seen as init.author.name) seed package author metadata and leak identity if committed in .npmrc. Remove these lines. (Also strip any _authToken in a committed .npmrc as a secret.)

rules.title: .idea/ JetBrains workspace files
rules.fix: JetBrains .idea/ files (especially workspace.xml) embed absolute local file paths, run configs, and user-specific state revealing the developer's home directory and username. Exclude the directory from the push.

rules.title: .vscode/ and *.code-workspace files with user paths
rules.fix: VS Code .vscode/settings.json and *.code-workspace files frequently contain absolute home-directory paths (python.defaultInterpreterPath, terminal cwd) that reveal the username. Scan contents for absolute /Users//home/C:\Users paths; some teams intentionally commit shared settings, so prefer content scrub over blanket removal (medium FP).

rules.title: Absolute macOS home path revealing username (/Users/<name>)
rules.fix: macOS absolute paths leak the local account name in the second segment. Rewrite to a relative path, $HOME, or ~ . Medium FP risk: '/Users/Shared', '/Users/Guest', and doc placeholders like '/Users/username' are not real identities; allowlist those tokens.

rules.title: Absolute Linux home path revealing username (/home/<name>)
rules.fix: Linux absolute paths leak the account name in the second segment. Rewrite to relative, $HOME, or ~ . Medium FP risk: service accounts and generic names ('/home/runner' on GitHub Actions, '/home/user', '/home/vagrant') are CI/placeholder, not personal; allowlist them.

rules.title: Absolute Windows home path revealing username (C:\Users\<name>)
rules.fix: Windows absolute paths leak the profile/account name after \Users\. Rewrite to %USERPROFILE% or a relative path. Medium FP risk: 'C:\Users\Public', 'C:\Users\Default', 'C:\Users\runneradmin' (GitHub Actions) are non-personal; allowlist them. Watch for forward-slash and double-escaped (C:\\Users\\) variants in JSON.

rules.title: Author/Artist/Copyright metadata embedded in committed images
rules.fix: Committed binary assets (JPEG/PNG/PDF/MP4) carry EXIF Artist, IPTC By-line, and XMP dc:creator / xmpRights:Owner fields naming the photographer/author (plus possible GPS). The proxy should run a metadata stripper (e.g. exiftool -all=) on committed assets rather than regex the raw bytes; this regex catches the embedded XMP/IPTC strings when present in text-extractable form.

rules.title: Committed media/asset files likely to carry author metadata
rules.fix: These formats commonly embed creator/author/last-modified-by metadata (EXIF/XMP/IPTC for images, Core Properties dc:creator/lastModifiedBy for OOXML, /Author for PDF, <dc:creator> for SVG). Route matched files through a metadata scrubber before push. High FP risk as a standalone signal: most matched files are benign, so use this only to select files for a metadata-strip pass, not to block.

rules.title: AI self-disclosure phrase ("As an AI language model")
rules.detect: \bas an? (?:ai|artificial intelligence)(?: language)?(?: model)?\b|\bas a large language model\b
rules.example: // As an AI language model, I cannot execute SQL queries on a live database.
rules.fix: Strip the line/comment entirely. This is verbatim model boilerplate that has no business in committed source; it should never reach the remote. Alert the developer that raw assistant output was pasted in.

rules.title: Assistant refusal opener ("I'm sorry, but I cannot")
rules.detect: \bi'?m sorry,? but\b|\bi (?:cannot|can'?t|am unable to|am not able to|do not have the ability to|don'?t have the capability to)\b.{0,60}\b(?:as an? ai|provide|access|execute|browse|generate)\b
rules.example: # I'm sorry, but I cannot provide the full implementation without more context.
rules.fix: Flag and remove. Genuine apologies in human comments are rare; pair with another LLM marker before auto-stripping to lower false positives. Tell the dev a refusal/apology fragment leaked into the diff.

rules.title: Conversational affirmation opener ("Certainly!" / "Sure!" / "Absolutely!")
rules.example: Certainly! Here is the refactored function you requested.
rules.fix: Warn. These are high-frequency LLM paragraph openers (Originality.AI flags "Certainly!"/"Absolutely!" as dead giveaways). When found at the start of a comment, commit body, or doc paragraph, surface for human review; remove if clearly chat residue.

rules.title: Chat handoff phrase ("Here's the ..." / "Here is the ...")
rules.example: Here's the updated configuration with the changes you asked for.
rules.fix: Warn only. "Here is the X" is a strong Claude/ChatGPT handoff opener but also appears in legitimate human docs. Require co-occurrence with a second marker ("you requested", "you asked for", "as requested") before flagging hard.

rules.title: LLM hedging connective ("Note that" / "It's important to note" / "Keep in mind")
rules.detect: \b(?:it'?s|it is) important to note\b|\bplease note that\b|\bkeep in mind that\b|\bit'?s worth noting\b
rules.example: It's important to note that this approach assumes the input is already validated.
rules.fix: Warn. Hallmark LLM hedging connectives over-represented in generated prose. Common in human docs too, so warn-only and rank by density (multiple hedges in one block = stronger signal).

rules.title: LLM summary/transition connectors ("In summary" / "In conclusion" / "Overall")
rules.detect: ^[\s>#/*-]*(?:in summary|in conclusion|to summarize|overall|to sum up|in essence)\b[,:]?
rules.example: // In summary, this module handles authentication and session refresh.
rules.fix: Warn. These closing connectors are statistically over-used by LLMs (DeepSeek/ChatGPT favor "in summary"). Surface when leading a comment or commit-message paragraph; benign in long-form READMEs.

rules.title: Training-data / knowledge-cutoff disclaimer
rules.detect: \bas of my (?:last )?(?:knowledge|training)(?: data)?(?: cut[- ]?off| update)?\b|\bmy (?:training data|knowledge) (?:cut[- ]?off|only goes up to|extends to)\b|\bi was (?:trained|last updated) (?:on|in)\b
rules.example: // As of my last knowledge update in 2023, the library did not support async iterators.
rules.fix: Strip the line. A training-cutoff disclaimer is unambiguous verbatim model output and can also leak a model-version/date fingerprint. Remove and alert.

rules.title: Assistant sign-off ("I hope this helps" / "Let me know if" / "Feel free to")
rules.detect: \bi hope this helps\b|\blet me know if (?:you|there|this|that)\b|\bfeel free to (?:ask|reach out|let me know|modify|adjust)\b|\bif you have any (?:other |further )?questions\b
rules.example: // I hope this helps! Let me know if you'd like me to add tests.
rules.fix: Strip. Conversational sign-offs are pure chat residue with no place in code, comments, or commit messages. Remove the sentence and flag the diff as containing pasted assistant output.

rules.title: LLM excess-vocabulary tells ("delve", "intricate", "underscore", "meticulous", "commendable")
rules.example: This change underscores our commitment to a robust and seamless authentication flow.
rules.fix: Info-only. These words are individually legitimate; only a cluster (3+ in one comment/commit) is a meaningful signal. Use density-based scoring, never single-word blocking. Per Liang et al. excess-vocabulary study these terms spiked post-ChatGPT.

rules.title: Generated placeholder secret ("your-api-key-here", YOUR_API_KEY)
rules.example: const apiKey = "your-api-key-here"; // replace with your actual key
rules.fix: Warn. Model-emitted placeholder credentials indicate copy-pasted sample code that was never wired to real config. Flag so the dev replaces with an env-var reference; also legitimate in docs, so don't auto-edit code blocks in *.md.

rules.title: Filler placeholder tokens (foo/bar/baz, lorem ipsum)
rules.example: function foo(bar) { return baz; } // lorem ipsum dolor sit amet
rules.fix: Info-only. foo/bar/lorem are ubiquitous in legitimate tests/examples; flag only when leaking into non-test production paths or alongside other LLM markers. Never block.

rules.title: example.com / example domain leftover from sample output
rules.fix: Info-only. example.com (RFC 2606 reserved) is standard in docs/tests; flag only in config/runtime defaults where it suggests an unfinished AI-generated stub. Do not flag in *.md or test fixtures.

rules.title: Model-stubbed TODO / placeholder implementation
rules.example: // TODO: implement the rest of the logic here
rules.fix: Warn. "rest of the code", "add your logic here", and "implement this" stubs are classic LLM elisions left when a model abbreviates a code block. Flag as incomplete/generated; the diff likely ships a non-functional stub.

rules.title: Code-elision marker ("// ... existing code ...", "<!-- rest unchanged -->")
rules.fix: Block/warn. These ellipsis-elision markers are emitted by code-assistants when showing a partial edit; if committed verbatim they delete real code. Strong signal of pasted assistant diff output - flag the diff as corrupted.

rules.title: Echoed prompt/instruction text in comment ("You are a helpful assistant", "Your task is to")
rules.detect: \byou are (?:a |an )?(?:helpful |senior |expert |professional )?(?:assistant|ai|developer|engineer|coding|programmer)\b|\byour task is to\b|\bact as (?:a|an)\b|\bplease (?:write|generate|create|provide)(?: me)? (?:a|the|some|code)\b|\bgiven the (?:following|above)\b.{0,40}\b(?:code|context|requirements)\b
rules.example: # You are a senior Python developer. Your task is to refactor the function below.
rules.fix: Warn. Role-priming / instruction text echoed into a comment means the prompt itself was pasted alongside output. Remove the instruction line; it leaks workflow (that an LLM was prompted) and sometimes proprietary prompt text.

rules.title: Chat-transcript role labels ("User:", "Assistant:", "### Response", chat template tokens)
rules.example:
# User: write a parser
# Assistant: Sure, here is a parser implementation:
rules.fix: Strip. Role labels and chat-template special tokens (<|im_start|>, <|assistant|>) are unmistakable transcript residue. Remove the transcript block; it both fingerprints the workflow and bloats the file.

rules.title: Stray markdown code fence committed inside a source file
rules.fix: Warn for non-markdown source files only (exclude *.md, *.mdx, *.rst, *.ipynb). A triple-backtick fence inside a .py/.js/.ts file is a sign the model's fenced answer was pasted wholesale. Suggest removing the fence lines.

rules.title: Conversational filler in commit message
rules.detect: ^(?:certainly|sure|absolutely|of course|here(?:'?s| is) (?:the|a|your)|i(?:'?ve| have) (?:made|updated|added|created)|i hope this|this commit (?:does|will|aims to)|as requested|as an ai)\b
rules.example: Certainly! Here's a commit message: Add input validation to the login handler
rules.fix: Warn/rewrite. Commit subjects should be imperative; conversational openers ("Certainly! Here's a commit message:", "I've updated...") betray AI generation. Strip the filler prefix and keep the imperative remainder.

rules.title: Commit message that quotes the prompt ("Here is a commit message:")
rules.example: Here is a concise commit message based on your changes: Refactor auth middleware
rules.fix: Block/rewrite. The literal phrase "Here is a commit message" means the assistant's framing was committed instead of just the message. Strip the framing line and any leading/trailing markdown; keep only the real subject/body.

rules.title: Doc/comment flagged as verbatim model output ("generated by ChatGPT/Claude")
rules.example: <!-- This documentation was generated by ChatGPT -->
rules.fix: Strip the attribution line. An explicit "generated by <model>" marker directly ties the artifact to an AI workflow - exactly the attribution the proxy is meant to scrub. Remove the comment/line.

rules.title: Boilerplate safety disclaimer ("consult a professional", "I am not a lawyer/doctor")
rules.detect: \b(?:please )?consult (?:a|with a|your) (?:qualified |licensed )?(?:professional|attorney|lawyer|physician|doctor|financial advisor|expert)\b|\bi(?:'?m| am) not a (?:lawyer|doctor|financial advisor|medical professional)\b|\bthis (?:is not|does not constitute) (?:legal|medical|financial) advice\b
rules.example: // Disclaimer: This is not legal advice. Please consult a qualified attorney.
rules.fix: Info-only. Unsolicited safety disclaimers in code comments are typical model hedging. Flag for review; legitimate in some compliance docs so do not auto-strip outside source files.

rules.title: Compile/generation timestamp embedded in generated files
rules.example: // Generated by protoc on 2026-06-20 14:32:07 +0530 -- do not edit
rules.fix: Code generators, doc builders, and bundlers stamp the wall-clock build time (often with the local timezone) into headers/footers of generated source, manpages, minified JS, and PDFs, leaking the same time-of-day and offset signals as commit metadata. Fix: honor SOURCE_DATE_EPOCH in the toolchain so generators emit a fixed quantized timestamp instead of 'now', or have the proxy redact matched timestamp tokens to a canonical UTC value; prefer omitting the timestamp line entirely. Medium FP: pattern can match legitimate intentional documentation dates.

rules.title: git-privacy encrypted original timestamp left in commit message
rules.fix: git-privacy can preserve the original (un-redacted) author/committer dates in encrypted form appended to the commit message so the developer can recover them. If that ciphertext ships to the remote, the real timestamps are recoverable by anyone with the key and the trailer itself signals git-privacy use. The anti-attribution proxy must strip any such recovery trailer before push and prefer running git-privacy without a stored key so only the reduced timestamp remains. Verify the exact trailer key against the installed git-privacy version, as it is configurable.

rules.title: Indentation style deviation (tabs vs spaces)
rules.fix: This is the CSFS 'tabsLeadLines' feature (Caliskan-Islam Table 3: whether the majority of indented lines begin with spaces or tabs). Canonicalize indentation to the configured indent_style. Run the language formatter (prettier for JS/TS/CSS/HTML/MD; black/pyproject.toml for Python -> spaces; gofmt for Go -> tabs; rustfmt for Rust -> spaces via tab_spaces). For unformatted file types, expand or convert leading whitespace per .editorconfig indent_style. Mixed tab+space leading whitespace (regex above) is the strongest tell and should always be flattened.

rules.title: Indentation width habit (2 vs 4 spaces)
rules.fix: Indent width is a per-author habit captured indirectly by ln(numSpaces/length) and whiteSpaceRatio. The regex flags space-leading lines that are not a multiple of the configured indent_size (e.g. 3/5/6/7 spaces when indent_size=2 or 4) as a cheap pre-filter; the authoritative check is diff against the formatter. Normalize to configured tabWidth/indent_size by running prettier (tabWidth), black (fixed 4), or rustfmt (tab_spaces, default 4). Honor .editorconfig indent_size and .prettierrc tabWidth as the canonical width.

rules.title: Trailing whitespace on lines
rules.fix: Trailing whitespace is an idiosyncratic, editor-driven habit and contributes to whiteSpaceRatio. Strip all trailing spaces/tabs from every line (respecting markdown two-space hard-break lines if Markdown is in scope). Every supported formatter removes trailing whitespace; for unformatted files apply a regex strip. Honor .editorconfig trim_trailing_whitespace=true as the canonical rule.

rules.title: Line-ending style (CRLF vs LF)
rules.fix: Line-ending style (CRLF vs LF vs CR) leaks the author's OS/editor and is a per-environment fingerprint. Normalize all EOLs to the configured end_of_line (default LF). Prettier's endOfLine option (default 'lf' since v2.0) and gofmt/black/rustfmt all emit LF. Honor .editorconfig end_of_line and .gitattributes 'text eol=' directives; do not rewrite files that .gitattributes pins to CRLF.

rules.title: Brace placement (Allman vs K&R / newline-before-open-brace)
rules.fix: This is the CSFS 'newLineBeforeOpenBrace' boolean (Caliskan-Islam Table 3: whether code-block braces are preceded by a newline) -- the Allman-vs-K&R tell. The regex flags an open brace on its own line after a closing paren. Canonicalize brace style to the formatter's mandated placement: prettier and gofmt enforce K&R (brace on the same line); rustfmt and black follow their language conventions. Brace style is non-configurable in prettier/gofmt, so simply running them flattens this feature. NOTE: this only normalizes the textual brace position; the underlying block structure (an AST/syntactic feature) is unchanged.

rules.title: Blank-line / empty-line patterns between statements
rules.fix: Frequency and grouping of blank lines is the CSFS 'ln(numEmptyLines/length)' feature and a strong per-author habit. The regex flags runs of 2+ consecutive blank lines (a common idiosyncratic pattern). Collapse consecutive blank lines to the formatter's canonical maximum: prettier collapses to at most 1 blank line; black/gofmt/rustfmt enforce their own blank-line rules between top-level and nested constructs. Running the formatter normalizes vertical spacing; for unformatted files, collapse 2+ blank lines to 1.

rules.title: Maximum line-length habit (overlong lines / wrap point)
rules.example: const result = computeSomethingVeryLong(argumentOne, argumentTwo, argumentThree, argumentFour, andMore);
rules.fix: Where an author chooses to wrap correlates with CSFS avgLineLength and stdDevLineLength (Table 2). The regex flags lines over a threshold (shown as >100) as a pre-filter; set the threshold to the configured printWidth. Re-wrap to the canonical width by running prettier (printWidth, default 80), black (line-length, default 88), or rustfmt (max_width, default 100). This normalizes the WRAP DECISIONS but not avgLineLength/stdDevLineLength fully, since semantic line counts still vary; treat as partial mitigation. High FP risk: URLs, long string literals, and import lines are legitimately long and should not be force-wrapped.

rules.title: String quote style (single vs double)
rules.fix: Quote-character preference (single vs double) is a stable per-author/per-editor habit. Canonicalize to the configured quote style by running prettier (singleQuote option, default false=double) for JS/TS/CSS, or black for Python (black normalizes to double quotes). The regex is an advisory pre-filter only -- it cannot distinguish string literals from apostrophes in comments/regex/template strings, so high FP risk; the formatter is the authoritative arbiter and is quote-aware. Honor .prettierrc singleQuote.

rules.title: Semicolon insertion habit (JS/TS)
rules.fix: Reliance on ASI (omitting trailing semicolons) vs always terminating is a JS/TS author habit. Canonicalize to the configured policy by running prettier (semi option, default true=add semicolons). The regex flags statement-like lines lacking a terminating semicolon as a pre-filter; high FP risk (multi-line expressions, object/array continuation). Prettier is AST-aware and is the authoritative normalizer. Honor .prettierrc semi.

rules.title: Import / include ordering and grouping
rules.example:
import zlib
import os
import requests
import sys
rules.fix: The chosen order and grouping of imports (alphabetical vs usage-order, stdlib/third-party/local grouping) is a deterministic per-author habit. Canonicalize import order: Python -> isort with profile='black' in pyproject.toml ([tool.isort]); Go -> goimports (groups stdlib / third-party / local, with -local for the local prefix); JS/TS -> ESLint sort-imports (--fix) or eslint-plugin-simple-import-sort. The regex flags consecutive import blocks as a pre-filter; whether they are mis-ordered is decided by the sorter. Re-sorting is semantics-preserving for side-effect-free imports; preserve relative order of side-effectful imports.

rules.title: Spacing around operators / after commas / inside brackets
rules.fix: Intra-line spacing habits (no space around '=', no space after ',', padding inside '()') feed the CSFS whiteSpaceRatio feature (Table 3, ratio of whitespace to non-whitespace). Canonicalize by running the language formatter, which enforces deterministic operator/comma/bracket spacing (prettier, black, gofmt, rustfmt all do this). The regex is a noisy pre-filter (matches inside strings/comments); the formatter is the authoritative, AST-aware normalizer of spacing.

rules.title: Identifier naming convention (camelCase vs snake_case)
rules.example: user_id and userName in the same file
rules.fix: Naming convention (camelCase vs snake_case vs PascalCase) is a strong per-author lexical tell and feeds CSFS WordUnigramTF (Table 2 word unigrams). HONEST CAVEAT: a normalization/formatting pass CANNOT safely fix this -- prettier/black/gofmt/rustfmt do NOT rename identifiers, and renaming requires full semantic refactoring (scope analysis, cross-file references, public API stability) that risks breaking code and is out of scope for a layout/lexical normalizer. Recommend FLAG-ONLY here: surface that identifiers deviate from a configured convention so the dev can decide, but do not auto-rewrite. This is one of the lexical signals that survives formatting.

rules.title: Abbreviation habits in identifiers (e.g. cnt, idx, tmp, btn)
rules.fix: Preferred abbreviations are an idiosyncratic lexical fingerprint contributing to WordUnigramTF n-grams. Like naming convention, this is NOT fixable by a formatter and renaming risks breaking code, so FLAG-ONLY: report abbreviation usage that deviates from a configured house dictionary. High FP risk -- these tokens appear inside legitimate longer identifiers and library names. This signal survives prettier/black/gofmt/rustfmt and is a residual leak the proxy cannot fully scrub without semantic rewriting.

rules.title: Comment density and comment style (// vs /* */, leading-space, capitalization)
rules.fix: Comment frequency (CSFS ln(numComments/length), Table 2) and micro-style (space after //, sentence capitalization, block vs line comments) are author tells. PARTIAL fix only: a formatter normalizes some comment spacing/indentation but does NOT change comment density or wording, and Caliskan-Islam note (p.258) that syntactic features are 'invariant to ... comments'. Recommend: optionally normalize comment marker spacing/case via a linter rule (e.g. ESLint capitalized-comments / spaced-comment) where configured, but recognize comment CONTENT and DENSITY remain a residual lexical leak. Stripping comments entirely is the only way to remove ln(numComments/length), which is usually undesirable and out of scope.
rules.source: https://www.usenix.org/conference/usenixsecurity15/technical-sessions/presentation/caliskan-islam (Table 2 ln(numComments/length); p.258 syntactic features invariant to comments)

rules.title: N-gram-detectable idioms and AST-residual stylometry (residual-leak warning)
rules.example: for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) { x += 1; }
rules.fix: Recurring token/idiom n-grams (loop idioms, ternary vs if/else, ++ vs +=1, null-check style) feed CSFS WordUnigramTF and, crucially, the AST-based features that are the STRONGEST attribution signals. HONEST CAVEAT (the central caveat for this channel): Caliskan-Islam state 'The AST node bigrams are the most discriminating features of all' (p.259) and that syntactic features are 'invariant to changes in source-code layout, as well as comments' (p.258). Therefore layout/lexical normalization (prettier/gofmt/black/rustfmt + whitespace/EOL/quote/import canonicalization) does NOT defeat ASTNodeBigramsTF, AST node TF/TFIDF, code-unigrams-in-AST-leaves, max AST depth, branching factor, or nesting depth. This rule exists to WARN the dev that residual AST/idiom stylometry persists after formatting; truly defeating it requires semantic source-to-source transformation (control-flow/AST rewriting, identifier renaming), which is out of scope for a normalization pass.
rules.source: https://www.usenix.org/conference/usenixsecurity15/technical-sessions/presentation/caliskan-islam (Table 4 ASTNodeBigramsTF 'most discriminating features of all', p.259; p.258 invariance statement); https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11150 (Horvath et al. 2026 survey)

rules.title: GitHub numeric-prefixed noreply email in author/committer identity (account-linkable)
rules.fix: Rewrite author/committer to a neutral anonymized identity. The integer before '+' is the GitHub account/app database ID and resolves to a public profile via api.github.com/user/{id}, re-identifying the person even though the address is 'noreply'. Applies to the author/committer slot (distinct from the co-authored-by trailer channel).

rules.title: Dependabot bot author/committer identity
rules.fix: Normalize the author/committer identity. A dependabot[bot] author reveals the repo uses GitHub-hosted dependency automation and that the commit is machine-generated.

rules.title: Renovate bot author/committer identity
rules.fix: Normalize the author/committer identity to remove the Renovate bot fingerprint.

rules.title: github-actions[bot] author/committer identity
rules.fix: Normalize identity. The fixed numeric ID 41898282 is the canonical github-actions app account and flags CI-generated commits.

rules.title: semantic-release-bot author/committer identity
rules.fix: Normalize identity to remove the release-automation fingerprint.

rules.title: GitHub web-flow committer (commit authored via GitHub web UI)
rules.fix: Rewrite committer. A 'GitHub <noreply@github.com>' committer reveals the commit was created/merged through GitHub's web interface rather than locally (behavioral workflow leak).

rules.title: GitLab project/group access-token bot identity
rules.fix: Normalize identity. The numeric project/group ID embedded in a GitLab bot email is account-linkable to a specific project.

rules.title: mergetag commit header embedding a signed tag's tagger identity + signature
rules.example:
mergetag object 5dc01c595e6c6ec9ccda4f6f69c131c0dd945f8c
type commit
tag v1.0
tagger Jane Dev <jane@corp.example> 1700000000 +0200
rules.fix: Strip the mergetag header from merge commits. When a signed tag is merged, git copies the entire tag object — including the tagger name, email, timezone offset, and PGP signature — into the merge commit's mergetag header.

rules.title: OpenCode Co-Authored-By trailer and 'Generated with opencode' footer
rules.fix: Strip the trailer and the 'Generated with opencode' line. OpenCode adds these to commits by default (configurable off).

rules.title: Charm Crush AI agent commit attribution
rules.fix: Strip Crush attribution from commit messages. Crush is Charm's terminal AI coding agent (successor to the Go OpenCode). Anchor matching to the trailer/footer context to avoid matching the English word 'crush'.

rules.title: Sourcegraph Amp agent commit attribution
rules.fix: Strip Amp attribution. Amp is Sourcegraph's agentic coding tool. Anchor to trailer context since 'Amp' is a short common token.

rules.title: GitLab Duo AI agent identity/attribution
rules.fix: Strip GitLab Duo attribution from commit messages and identity fields.

rules.title: AI code-review/PR bots (Sweep, Korbit, Ellipsis, CodeRabbit) author identity
rules.fix: Normalize identity to remove the AI review-bot fingerprint.

rules.title: Gerrit Change-Id trailer (workflow/review-system fingerprint)
rules.fix: Strip the Change-Id trailer. It reveals the project uses Gerrit code review and links the commit to a specific change/review thread traceable to a person.

rules.title: Issue auto-close keyword in commit message (Closes/Fixes/Resolves #N)
rules.fix: Optionally generalize or strip the issue reference. An auto-close keyword + issue number links the commit to a tracked workflow item traceable to the person who filed/owns it. Distinct from the draft's generic ticket-reference rule because the keyword form actively mutates issue state and is a stronger workflow signal.

rules.title: Committed OS artifact files leaking username/locale (.DS_Store, Thumbs.db, desktop.ini)
rules.fix: Block/strip these files. .DS_Store embeds Finder metadata and local paths; desktop.ini and Thumbs.db leak Windows usernames, locale, and folder structure.

rules.title: Committed editor swap/backup files (vim .swp, *~, .orig) leaking user@host
rules.fix: Block/strip editor temp files. Vim swap (.swp) files embed the editing user's login name and hostname in their header; *~ and .#file are emacs/editor backups revealing local workflow.

rules.title: Vim/Emacs modeline in file content (editor + personal-config fingerprint)
rules.example: # vim: set ts=4 sw=4 et ft=python :
rules.fix: Optionally normalize/strip modelines. They reveal the author's editor (vim vs emacs) and personal indentation/encoding preferences (a stylometric tell).

rules.title: Committed .netrc / _netrc credentials file (username + host leak)
rules.fix: Block .netrc files. They contain machine/login/password lines exposing the developer's username and the hosts they authenticate to.

rules.title: CITATION.cff / .zenodo.json author roster (names, ORCID, affiliation)
rules.fix: Strip or anonymize author entries. CITATION.cff and .zenodo.json contain full author given/family names, email, ORCID iDs, and affiliations — a richer identity roster than AUTHORS files.

rules.title: git notes ref content (refs/notes) carrying out-of-band author metadata
rules.fix: Do not push refs/notes/* through the proxy unless sanitized. Git notes are separate objects (with their own committer identity + timestamp) attached to commits, often carrying review/deployment metadata traceable to people; they are not transferred by default but can leak when explicitly pushed.

rules.title: Committed SSH allowed_signers / gpg key files (public-key fingerprint roster)
rules.fix: Review/strip. An allowed_signers file maps each contributor's email to their SSH signing public key, and committed .gpg/.asc/.pub files expose key fingerprints that re-identify authors across repos.

rules.title: .editorconfig encodes editor/style setup fingerprint
rules.fix: .editorconfig encodes indentation/charset/EOL habits; review whether it reveals a personal setup.

rules.title: .gitattributes encodes toolchain setup
rules.fix: .gitattributes (filters/diff/merge drivers) can fingerprint a toolchain; review before publishing.

rules.title: .gitmodules reveals upstream/source relationships
rules.fix: Submodule URLs can reveal private infrastructure or identity; review before publishing.
